_comment: Canned static responses for the 8 evaluation-domain conditions, keyed by intent then canonical condition key. Used when EMMA_USE_RAG=false. Edit values here without touching Python.

getsymptoms.heart_attack:
Classic symptoms of a heart attack include:
• Crushing or pressure-like chest pain, often radiating to the left arm, jaw, or back
• Shortness of breath (dyspnea)
• Diaphoresis (profuse sweating)
• Nausea and vomiting
• Lightheadedness or syncope

Note: Women and diabetic patients may present atypically — fatigue, epigastric pain, or nausea without chest pain.
getsymptoms.stroke:
Use the FAST mnemonic:
• Face drooping (asymmetric smile)
• Arm weakness (unilateral)
• Speech difficulty (slurred or absent)
• Time to call emergency services

Additional features: sudden severe headache ('thunderclap'), vision changes, ataxia, or altered consciousness.
getsymptoms.pulmonary_embolism:
Classic triad (present together in <30% of cases):
• Haemoptysis (coughing blood)
• Pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worsens with breathing)
• Dyspnoea (sudden onset shortness of breath)

Also: tachycardia, tachypnoea, leg swelling/pain (DVT), and in massive PE — haemodynamic collapse.
getsymptoms.sepsis:
Sepsis is defined by the SOFA criteria but clinically look for:
• Fever (>38 °C) or hypothermia (<36 °C)
• Tachycardia (HR >90 bpm)
• Tachypnoea (RR >20)
• Altered mental status
• Signs of organ dysfunction: oliguria, jaundice, mottled skin

Septic shock = sepsis + refractory hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg) despite fluids.
getsymptoms.anaphylaxis:
Anaphylaxis is a multi-system hypersensitivity reaction:
• Skin/mucosa: urticaria, flushing, angioedema
• Respiratory: bronchospasm, stridor, laryngeal oedema
• Cardiovascular: tachycardia, hypotension, shock
• GI: nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps

Onset is typically rapid (minutes) after allergen exposure.
getsymptoms.meningitis:
Classic triad of bacterial meningitis:
• Severe headache
• Neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity)
• Fever

Additional signs: photophobia, phonophobia, Kernig's sign, Brudzinski's sign, altered consciousness, and petechial rash (indicates meningococcal disease — a medical emergency).
getsymptoms.appendicitis:
Typical presentation:
• Periumbilical pain migrating to the right iliac fossa (McBurney's point)
• Anorexia and nausea
• Low-grade fever
• Rebound tenderness and guarding

Atypical in pregnancy, elderly, and retrocaecal appendix.
getsymptoms.diabetic_ketoacidosis:
DKA triad: hyperglycaemia + ketosis + metabolic acidosis

Symptoms:
• Polyuria and polydipsia
• Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
• Kussmaul breathing (deep, laboured)
• Fruity (acetone) breath
• Dehydration and weakness
• Altered consciousness in severe cases

getdiagnosis.heart_attack:
Diagnostic workup for MI:
1. ECG — ST elevation (STEMI) or depression/T-wave changes (NSTEMI)
2. Troponin I/T — rises within 3-4 h, peaks at 12-24 h (use serial measurements)
3. CK-MB — less sensitive but useful for timing re-infarction
4. CXR — rule out aortic dissection; check for pulmonary oedema
5. Echo — assess wall motion abnormalities and EF
6. Coronary angiography — definitive (and therapeutic) for STEMI
getdiagnosis.stroke:
Immediate stroke workup:
1. Non-contrast CT head — first-line: rules out haemorrhage before thrombolysis
2. MRI/DWI — more sensitive for early ischaemia
3. CT angiography — identify large vessel occlusion for thrombectomy
4. Blood glucose — hypoglycaemia mimics stroke
5. Labs: CBC, INR, aPTT, electrolytes, troponin
getdiagnosis.pulmonary_embolism:
PE diagnostic pathway:
1. Pre-test probability: Well's score
2. D-dimer — high sensitivity; negative rules out PE in low-probability patients
3. CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) — gold standard for diagnosis
4. V/Q scan — if CTPA contraindicated (e.g., renal failure, contrast allergy)
5. Echo — right heart strain in massive PE
6. Lower-limb Doppler — confirms DVT when imaging is equivocal
getdiagnosis.sepsis:
Sepsis workup:
1. Blood cultures ×2 (before antibiotics if possible)
2. CBC — leukocytosis or leukopenia, thrombocytopenia
3. Lactate — >2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion
4. CRP, procalcitonin — inflammatory markers
5. Urine culture, CXR, or source-specific imaging
6. BMP, LFTs, coagulation — organ dysfunction assessment
getdiagnosis.anaphylaxis:
Anaphylaxis is a clinical diagnosis — do not delay treatment for labs.

Supportive investigations:
• Serum tryptase — draw within 1-2 h of onset; elevated confirms mast-cell activation
• Allergen-specific IgE (skin-prick or RAST) — done electively after recovery
• ABG if respiratory compromise
• ECG if cardiovascular involvement
getdiagnosis.meningitis:
Meningitis workup:
1. Lumbar puncture (LP) — CSF analysis is definitive:
   • Bacterial: cloudy, ↑WBC (neutrophils), ↑protein, ↓glucose
   • Viral: clear, ↑lymphocytes, normal glucose
2. CT head before LP if papilloedema, focal neuro signs, or immunocompromised
3. Blood cultures — before antibiotics
4. PCR (CSF/blood) — rapid bacterial/viral identification
5. CBC, CRP, procalcitonin, glucose
getdiagnosis.appendicitis:
Appendicitis workup:
1. Clinical assessment — Alvarado score
2. CBC — leukocytosis with left shift
3. CRP — elevated
4. Urine dipstick — rule out UTI
5. Ultrasound — first-line (avoids radiation), especially in children/pregnancy
6. CT abdomen/pelvis — gold standard for adults when ultrasound inconclusive
7. MRI — preferred in pregnancy
getdiagnosis.diabetic_ketoacidosis:
DKA diagnostic criteria:
• Blood glucose >11 mmol/L (>200 mg/dL)
• pH <7.3 and/or HCO₃ <18 mmol/L
• Ketonaemia (β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L) or ketonuria

Workup:
1. BMP — glucose, electrolytes, BUN/creatinine
2. VBG or ABG — pH and bicarbonate
3. Urinalysis — ketones
4. CBC, cultures — identify precipitating infection
5. ECG — hyperkalaemia (peaked T waves)

gettreatment.heart_attack:
STEMI management (time-critical):
1. Dual antiplatelet: Aspirin 325 mg + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor/clopidogrel)
2. Anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin or LMWH
3. Primary PCI — goal door-to-balloon <90 min (preferred)
4. Thrombolysis — if PCI unavailable within 120 min
5. Oxygen — only if SpO₂ <90%
6. Nitrates — for ongoing pain (contraindicated in inferior MI + RV involvement)
7. Beta-blocker, ACEi, statin — initiated early and continued long-term
gettreatment.stroke:
Ischaemic stroke (if eligible):
1. IV tPA (alteplase) — within 4.5 h of symptom onset, if no contraindications
2. Mechanical thrombectomy — for large vessel occlusion, up to 24 h
3. Aspirin 300 mg — within 24 h if no thrombolysis
4. Blood pressure management — permissive hypertension (target <185/110 before tPA)

Haemorrhagic stroke:
• Reverse anticoagulation immediately
• Neurosurgical consultation for clot evacuation
gettreatment.pulmonary_embolism:
PE management by severity:
• Low-risk: direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC — rivaroxaban or apixaban)
• Submassive (RV strain without shock): anticoagulation ± catheter-directed thrombolysis
• Massive (haemodynamic instability): systemic thrombolysis (tPA) or surgical embolectomy

Anticoagulation duration:
• Provoked: 3 months
• Unprovoked/recurrent: indefinite
gettreatment.sepsis:
Sepsis 'Hour-1 Bundle' (Surviving Sepsis Campaign):
1. Measure lactate — repeat if initial >2 mmol/L
2. Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics
3. Broad-spectrum antibiotics — within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
4. 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid bolus for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
5. Vasopressors (norepinephrine) if MAP <65 mmHg despite fluids
6. Source control — drain abscess, remove infected device
gettreatment.anaphylaxis:
Anaphylaxis treatment:
1. IM Epinephrine 0.5 mg (1:1000) — anterolateral thigh, IMMEDIATELY
   (may repeat every 5-15 min as needed)
2. Call for emergency help; place patient supine with legs elevated
3. High-flow O₂; prepare for intubation if airway threatened
4. IV access + fluid resuscitation for hypotension
5. Antihistamines (H1 + H2) and corticosteroids — adjuncts only, not first-line
6. Observe ≥6 h for biphasic reaction
7. Prescribe auto-injector EpiPen on discharge
gettreatment.meningitis:
Bacterial meningitis — treat empirically before culture results:
1. IV Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h — reduces neurological complications
2. IV Antibiotics (within 30 min of diagnosis):
   • Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h ± ampicillin (if Listeria risk: immunocompromised, elderly)
   • Add vancomycin if penicillin-resistant pneumococcus suspected
3. Supportive: analgesia, antipyretics, IVF
4. Isolate (droplet precautions) for meningococcal disease
5. Prophylaxis: rifampicin or ciprofloxacin for close contacts
gettreatment.appendicitis:
Appendicitis management:
1. Surgical: laparoscopic appendectomy — gold standard
   • Emergency if perforated or peritonitis
2. Non-operative: IV antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanate or pip-tazo)
   • Selected uncomplicated cases; ~30% failure rate at 5 years
3. Pre-op: IV fluids, analgesia (opioids safe — don't mask diagnosis),
   NBM, antibiotics within 1 h of surgery
gettreatment.diabetic_ketoacidosis:
DKA management:
1. IV Fluids — 0.9% NaCl: 1 L in first hour, then titrate
2. Insulin — fixed-rate IV infusion (0.1 U/kg/h) after potassium >3.5 mmol/L
3. Potassium replacement — monitor hourly; add KCl to fluids if K⁺ <5.5
4. Glucose monitoring — switch to 10% dextrose when glucose <14 mmol/L
5. Identify and treat precipitant — infection, missed insulin, new T1DM
6. Resolution criteria: pH >7.3, HCO₃ >18, ketones <0.6 mmol/L
7. Transition to subcutaneous insulin 30-60 min before stopping infusion

getriskfactors.heart_attack:
Major modifiable risk factors:
• Hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, diabetes mellitus
• Smoking, obesity, physical inactivity
• Diet high in saturated fat

Non-modifiable:
• Age (men >45, women >55)
• Family history of premature CAD (<55 M, <65 F)
• Male sex (premenopausal women are relatively protected)
getriskfactors.stroke:
Modifiable: hypertension (single greatest risk factor), atrial fibrillation, hyperlipidaemia, diabetes, smoking, heavy alcohol use, obesity, carotid stenosis

Non-modifiable: age, male sex, Black ethnicity (higher stroke incidence), family history, prior TIA or stroke
getriskfactors.pulmonary_embolism:
Virchow's triad:
1. Stasis — prolonged immobilisation, long-haul travel, post-operative
2. Hypercoagulability — malignancy, thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid syndrome), OCP, HRT, pregnancy
3. Endothelial injury — surgery, trauma, central venous catheters
getriskfactors.sepsis:
Host factors: extremes of age (neonates, elderly), immunosuppression (HIV, chemotherapy, steroids), diabetes, chronic organ failure

Healthcare-associated: invasive devices (catheters, ventilators), surgery, ICU admission, antibiotic-resistant organisms
getriskfactors.anaphylaxis:
Previous anaphylaxis is the strongest predictor of future reactions.

Common triggers:
• Foods: peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, fish, milk, egg
• Drugs: beta-lactams (penicillin), NSAIDs, contrast media
• Insect venom: bee, wasp stings
• Latex

Cofactors that amplify risk: exercise, alcohol, NSAIDs, menstruation
getriskfactors.meningitis:
Bacterial meningitis risk factors:
• Age: neonates (GBS, E. coli), young adults (Neisseria meningitidis), elderly (Listeria, Streptococcus pneumoniae)
• Immunocompromised state (HIV, asplenia, complement deficiency)
• Crowded living conditions (university dormitories, military barracks)
• Unvaccinated status (meningococcal, pneumococcal, Hib vaccines)
• CSF leak, cochlear implant, neurosurgical procedures
getriskfactors.appendicitis:
• Age: peak 10-30 years
• Male sex (M:F ratio ~1.4:1)
• Low-fibre diet (may contribute via faecalith formation)
• Family history (genetic predisposition suggested)
• Previous GI infections
getriskfactors.diabetic_ketoacidosis:
• Type 1 diabetes (most common substrate)
• Precipitants:
  — Infection (most common: UTI, pneumonia)
  — Missed or inadequate insulin doses
  — New-onset T1DM (DKA may be the presenting event)
  — Myocardial infarction, pancreatitis, surgery
  — Certain drugs: SGLT-2 inhibitors (euglycaemic DKA), corticosteroids

geturgency.heart_attack:
STEMI is a time-critical emergency.

• Door-to-balloon time target: <90 minutes
• Each 30-min delay adds ~7.5% relative increase in 1-year mortality
• Without treatment, mortality approaches 30-40%
• With timely PCI, in-hospital mortality is ~5%

Call emergency services immediately — do not self-transport.
geturgency.stroke:
'Time is brain' — approximately 1.9 million neurons die every minute of untreated stroke.

• IV thrombolysis window: 4.5 hours from symptom onset
• Thrombectomy window: up to 24 hours for selected patients
• Door-to-needle target for tPA: <60 minutes

Disability can be dramatically reduced with rapid treatment — every 15-min improvement in treatment time saves one in seven patients from death or disability.
geturgency.pulmonary_embolism:
Urgency depends on severity:
• Massive PE (obstructive shock): 30-day mortality 30-65% — immediate resuscitation
• Submassive PE (RV strain, no shock): intermediate-risk; close monitoring required
• Low-risk PE: can be managed with outpatient anticoagulation (HESTIA criteria)

Overall case fatality: ~15% untreated; reduced to ~2-8% with treatment.
geturgency.sepsis:
Sepsis is a medical emergency with time-sensitive mortality.

• Each hour delay in antibiotics increases mortality by ~7-8%
• Septic shock has ~40-50% in-hospital mortality
• Sepsis kills more people annually than breast, bowel, and prostate cancer combined

Implement the Hour-1 Bundle immediately upon recognition.
geturgency.anaphylaxis:
Anaphylaxis can be fatal within minutes without treatment.

• Death typically from asphyxia (airway oedema) or cardiovascular collapse
• IM epinephrine must be given immediately — delays worsen outcomes
• Biphasic reactions occur in up to 20% of cases (6-72 h after initial reaction)
• Minimum 6-hour observation period recommended
geturgency.meningitis:
Bacterial meningitis is a neurological emergency.

• Mortality: up to 25% even with treatment
• Neurological sequelae (deafness, cognitive impairment) in 20-30% of survivors
• Antibiotics should be given within 30 minutes of clinical suspicion — do not wait for LP if it would cause delay
• Meningococcal meningitis with purpuric rash = IV benzylpenicillin immediately
geturgency.appendicitis:
Appendicitis is urgent but typically not immediately life-threatening.

• Risk of perforation increases significantly after 72 hours of symptoms
• Perforation rate: ~20-30% overall; higher in children and elderly
• Perforated appendicitis has significantly higher morbidity and mortality
• Early appendectomy (within 12-24 h of diagnosis) is associated with best outcomes
geturgency.diabetic_ketoacidosis:
DKA is a life-threatening metabolic emergency.

• Untreated mortality: up to 5-10% (higher in elderly, delayed presentation)
• Common causes of death: hypokalaemia-induced arrhythmia, cerebral oedema (especially in children)
• ICU-level monitoring may be required for severe DKA (pH <7.1)
• Reversal typically occurs within 12-24 h with appropriate treatment

getdifferentiation.heart_attack:
Heart attack vs. Stable angina:
• Angina: chest pain at exertion, resolves with rest/GTN, no troponin rise
• MI: rest pain, prolonged (>20 min), troponin elevation, ECG changes

Heart attack vs. Aortic dissection:
• Dissection: tearing pain radiating to back, pulse differential, CXR widened mediastinum
• MI: crushing pain, ± ST changes — but check: thrombolytics are dangerous in dissection
getdifferentiation.stroke:
Ischaemic vs. Haemorrhagic stroke:
• Ischaemic (85%): onset with activity, progressive; CT may be normal early
• Haemorrhagic (15%): sudden severe headache ('worst of life'), vomiting, decreased GCS; CT shows hyperdense lesion immediately

Stroke vs. TIA:
• TIA: symptoms fully resolve within 24 h (usually <1 h); MRI may still show infarct
• Stroke: persistent neurological deficit

Stroke mimics: hypoglycaemia (always check glucose!), Todd's paresis, complex migraine, hypertensive encephalopathy
getdifferentiation.pulmonary_embolism:
PE vs. Pneumothorax:
• Pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, may have trauma history
• PE: normal/reduced breath sounds, pleural rub, DVT risk factors

PE vs. Acute coronary syndrome:
• ACS: ST changes, troponin may be elevated in PE (from RV strain — but higher in ACS)
• PE: S1Q3T3 on ECG (though non-specific), right heart strain on echo
getdifferentiation.sepsis:
Sepsis vs. Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS):
• SIRS: fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea, leukocytosis — from ANY cause (not just infection)
• Sepsis: SIRS + documented or suspected infection + organ dysfunction (SOFA ≥2)

Septic shock vs. Cardiogenic shock:
• Septic: warm extremities, bounding pulse, high CO (initially)
• Cardiogenic: cold peripheries, elevated JVP, pulmonary oedema, low CO
getdifferentiation.anaphylaxis:
Anaphylaxis vs. Allergic reaction:
• Allergic: localised (rash, rhinitis); no systemic compromise
• Anaphylaxis: multi-system; cardiovascular or respiratory compromise

Anaphylaxis vs. Vasovagal syncope:
• Vasovagal: bradycardia, no urticaria, triggers (pain, blood), rapid recovery supine
• Anaphylaxis: tachycardia, urticaria/angioedema, allergen exposure

Anaphylaxis vs. Hereditary angioedema (HAE):
• HAE: no urticaria, no response to epinephrine, complement C4 low
getdifferentiation.meningitis:
Bacterial vs. Viral meningitis:
• Bacterial: higher fever, more toxic, CSF neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein; mortality 25% without treatment
• Viral: milder, CSF lymphocytic, normal glucose; usually self-limiting

Meningitis vs. Subarachnoid haemorrhage:
• SAH: sudden thunderclap headache, trauma history; CT positive; no fever (unless secondary meningitis develops)
• Meningitis: fever + neck stiffness + headache developing over hours-days
getdifferentiation.appendicitis:
Appendicitis vs. Ovarian pathology (in women):
• Ovarian torsion: acute adnexal pain, vomiting, US shows enlarged ovary
• Ectopic: positive beta-hCG, peritoneal signs, shoulder-tip pain

Appendicitis vs. Mesenteric adenitis:
• Adenitis (common in children): less localised pain, recent URTI, CT shows lymph nodes without appendix involvement

Appendicitis vs. Right-sided UTI/pyelonephritis:
• UTI: dysuria, positive urinalysis, no RIF guarding
getdifferentiation.diabetic_ketoacidosis:
DKA vs. Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS):
• DKA: T1DM typical, glucose <30 mmol/L, significant acidosis and ketonemia
• HHS: T2DM typical, glucose often >30 mmol/L, minimal ketones, profound dehydration, higher mortality

DKA vs. Lactic acidosis:
• Lactic acidosis: anion gap metabolic acidosis, lactate >5 mmol/L, no ketonaemia, glucose may be normal

Euglycaemic DKA: glucose <11 mmol/L — seen with SGLT-2 inhibitors; easy to miss without ketone measurement
